summary: Linked `src/melee/ft/ftdevice.c` by switching it from NonMatching to Matching, tightening its split ranges, and correcting symbol visibility/ownership for literals and BSS/SBSS objects. The C source removed forced-active literal externs, made `ft_804D6570` global instead of static, and exported it in the header, allowing the object to match without artificial retention pragmas.
what_changed: `config/GALE01/splits.txt` reduced the `ftdevice.c` BSS split from `0x80459A68-0x8045A6C0` to `0x80459A68-0x80459A98`, indicating ownership of only the local `ftdevice` BSS range.

`config/GALE01/symbols.txt` converted several `ftDevice_*` and `ftCo_*` string symbols in `.data`/`.sdata` from named global objects to compiler-style local labels such as `@249`, `@250`, `@251`, `@261`, and `@271`.

`config/GALE01/symbols.txt` collapsed the BSS entries at `0x80459A68`, `0x80459A74`, and `0x80459A8C` into a single global object `ft_80459A68` of size `0x30`, with a local `...bss.0` label at the same start address.
`src/melee/ft/ftdevice.c` removed `#pragma force_active on` and removed extern declarations for literal strings previously forced active.
`src/melee/ft/ftdevice.c` changed `ft_804D6570` from `static int` to global `int ft_804D6570`, and reordered it after `ftDevice_BuryThingCount` in source.
`src/melee/ft/ftdevice.h` added an extern declaration for `ft_804D6570` at SBSS address comment `4D6570`.
smart_moves: Removed artificial `#pragma force_active` usage once the literals could be represented as local compiler-emitted symbols, which is cleaner for a linked/matching object.
Adjusted symbol scope instead of preserving misleading global names for local string literals; this keeps `symbols.txt` closer to what the compiler/linker actually emits.
Fixed BSS range ownership in `splits.txt` so `ftdevice.c` no longer claims a much larger region extending to `0x8045A6C0`.
Made `ft_804D6570` non-static and exported it in the header, aligning source linkage with the symbol map requirement for matching.
decomp_lessons: When linking a formerly nonmatching object, verify not only `.text` but also `.data`, `.sdata`, `.bss`, and `.sbss` ownership in `splits.txt` and `symbols.txt`.
Do not keep placeholder global names for string literals if evidence shows they are local compiler labels; converting them to `@NNN` local symbols can be necessary for link correctness.
Avoid `#pragma force_active` as a crutch once the object can naturally emit or reference the needed literals.
Static-vs-global storage class matters for matching: a variable declared `static` in C may need to become a global extern if the expected symbol has global scope.
Adjacent BSS variables may need to be represented as one array/object if that matches the emitted layout better than several guessed individual symbols.
naming_conventions: Compiler-local literals are represented as `@NNN` symbols in `symbols.txt` with `scope:local`.
Unknown globals retain address-based names such as `ft_80459A68` and `ft_804D6570` when no semantic name is established.
Fighter common functions in this area use the `ftCo_` prefix, e.g. `ftCo_800C0658`, `ftCo_800C06E8`, `ftCo_800C0764`, and `ftCo_800C07F8`.
Device-related unknown structs use provisional names such as `ftDeviceUnk3`, `ftDeviceUnk4`, and `ftDeviceUnk5`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Switch the object to `Matching` only after source, symbol scopes, and split ranges are consistent.
Use local labels for compiler-generated string literals instead of extern literal declarations in C.
Represent BSS layout as `struct ftDeviceUnk3 ft_80459A68[4]` covering `0x30` bytes, matching the symbol map collapse.
Export SBSS variable `ft_804D6570` rather than keeping it file-local, because the symbol table expects a global object at `0x804D6570`.
follow_up_queries: Find other matching PRs that remove `#pragma force_active` after converting literal symbols to local `@NNN` labels.
Search for other fighter BSS split corrections where several guessed BSS symbols are collapsed into one array/object.
Inspect future `ftdevice` cleanup for whether `ft_80459A68`, `ft_804D6570`, and `ftDeviceUnk*` receive semantic names.
